post: Last week, BRI had a party to celebrate America's 250th birthday and the release of Divided Over the Declaration, the new book by David Bobb and Tony Williams about 250 years of debate over the meaning and legacy of the Declaration of Independence. David and Tony signed copies, guests loved the photo booth, and BRI's best-dressed employee, Echo the Golden Retriever, stole the show. In between all of that, something happened that's easy to miss in photos but hard to miss in person: a room full of people who believe the story of America's founding is still worth telling, and our principles still worth arguing! Thank you for everyone who joined us for a lovely evening! Divided Over the Declaration is out now:  https://bit.ly/4oL7HKy  #A250  #DividedOvertheDeclaration

The phrase "in all cases whatsoever" is what makes Grievance 22 so alarming. Parliament declared itself empowered to legislate for the colonies without exception, while simultaneously suspending the colonial legislatures that had represented colonists for generations. Sasha Rickard of MIT Concourse explains Grievance 22 through John Locke's framewor. Locke argued that anyone who attempts to gain absolute power over another "must be understood to be acting aggressively," and that resistance is justified even before harm occurs. Simply by claiming unlimited legislative authority, Parliament had already committed what Locke called an act of war. Britain had claimed unlimited power and dismantled the only institution capable of checking it. For colonists reasoning from Lockean principles, this wasn't a policy dispute. It was a government that had declared war on the governed, and justified the governed in responding. Discover more and join us for Constitution Day Live:  https://bit.ly/4uOsIoM  #DeclarationOfIndependence  #AmericanHistory  #CivicsEducation  #HistoryTeachers  #JohnLocke

NEW Investor Member Spotlight! ✨ From Sea to Shining Sea: Bill of Rights Institute Advancing Civic Education Across Pennsylvania and America Read more:  https://ow.ly/j3FO50Zf8wE via My FrontPage Story

The Massachusetts Bay Colonial Charter was signed in 1629. For 145 years, it served as the contractual foundation of colonial self-governance, defining the relationship between the colony and the Crown, establishing how government would operate, and securing rights colonists had come to understand as theirs. In 1774, Parliament rewrote it without asking. The Massachusetts Government Act made unilateral changes to a charter that had been renegotiated once before  in 1691, following the Glorious Revolution  through a process that reflected the mutual relationship between colony and Crown. That precedent made the 1774 Act more than a policy change. It was a breach of the governing framework itself. Kelsa Pelletiere of the University of Mississippi explains why Grievance 21 targets this act specifically: the consent of the governed isn't just a principle that applies to individual laws. It applies to the structures of government itself. When Parliament changed those structures without colonial approval, it signaled that colonial self-governance had never been truly secure, that it existed only at the Crown's pleasure. Episode 21 of our 27-part series is live. Join us for Constitution Day Live:  https://bit.ly/42cJB0M  #DeclarationOfIndependence  #AmericanHistory  #CivicsEducation  #HistoryTeachers  #USHistory

On June 19, 1865, Union soldiers rode into Galveston, Texas, and read General Order No. 3 aloud. The last enslaved Americans in Texas learned they were free. It had been two and a half years since the Emancipation Proclamation. Months since the Confederate surrender. The 13th Amendment, formally abolishing slavery throughout the United States, would be ratified six months later. But June 19, 1865 is the day freedom moved from proclamation to practice for the last people who had been waiting for it. The Declaration of Independence declared in 1776 that all people are created equal. That liberty is not a privilege granted by governments but a right inherent to human dignity. Juneteenth is the honest measure of how long it took for that founding promise to reach every American. That measure is not a reason to abandon the principles. It is a reason to teach them carefully, truthfully, and with full respect for the distance between America's ideals and the moments when it failed to live up to them. This is what BRI means when we say we teach the whole story of America, including when we lived up to our ideals, and when we fell catastrophically short. Juneteenth is essential to that story. It is a day that belongs in every history and civics classroom in the country. Today, we honor the courage and resilience of those who survived slavery, and we recommit to the work of civic education that takes seriously both the promise of the founding and the full history of what it took to pursue it. Explore BRI's Juneteenth educational resources 👇  https://bit.ly/4w1FhhS  #OTD  #Juneteenth  #TeachHistory  #AmericanHistory  #BlackHistory  #BRI  #AmericaAt250  #Justice

The skills employers say they need most right now: Critical thinking. Effective communication. Collaboration. Managing disagreement. Ethical reasoning. These skills are civics outcomes. That's the case BRI Director of Strategic Partnerships Jennifer Seal makes, and it's exactly why BRI recently became a proud Investor Member of the PA Chamber of Business and Industry. "Civics provides the knowledge, skills, and habits needed to be responsible and productive  and ones employers are consistently seeking," Seal said. "We believe civics must be prioritized in schools to prepare students to engage in the democratic process, improve their communities, and succeed in their careers." Pennsylvania is a fitting home for this partnership. The United States was established in Philadelphia. The Union was preserved at Gettysburg. Coal, steel, and railroads helped modernize America. The Keystone State has always been at the center of the American story, and BRI is committed to making sure the next generation knows that story and is equipped to carry it forward. 16 days until July 4, 2026. The 250th anniversary is a call to invest in the next 250 years. Read the full piece 👇  https://bit.ly/4uNgA7Q  #PAChamber  #BRI  #Pennysylvania  #A250

250 years ago, the American colonists were trying to figure out what to call themselves. On his own rough cut of the Declaration, Jefferson didnt pen the word citizens.  He wrote "subjects.  And then he crossed out that word and wrote citizens  instead. For well over two centuries, no one knew that Jefferson had first inked the word subjects.  In 2010, the Library of Congress used imaging technology to scrape back the layers of ink. First subjects. Then citizens. That shiftfrom a people defined by power over them to a people who have rights because they are humansis the Declaration's whole argument. And it's where Tony Williams and I started when we sat down with Major Garrett on The Takeout. We talked about what that change set in motion, what it actually cost to move the country closer to its own promise, and why the argument it started is the one that still matters most. Link to the episode in the first comment.

NBC News asked a good question: Do we have the civic education we need for America's 250th? I joined Jon Erwin, the director of Young Washington, to talk about what were doing to make sure the answer is Yes!  The filmhitting theaters July 3rdtells the story of the George Washington you don't know. Not the man of marble but the young man who had to learn from his failures. Young Washington is the center of a new educational initiative the Wonder Project, Stand Together, and the Bill of Rights Institute are bringing to families and classrooms nationwide. The idea is simple: you can't love what you don't know. And a great story is the best way to spark curiosity. Link to the full segment in the first comment.

Grievance 20 is the only grievance in the Declaration aimed at a law passed for another colony. That tells you something about the nature of the fear. The Quebec Act of 1774 replaced English common law with French civil law in Quebec, created a representative system where the king, not the people, appointed the members, and expanded Canadian territory into what is now the Midwest. Individually, each of those provisions alarmed the colonists. Together, they produced something worse: a model of arbitrary government established within immediate proximity to the American colonies. Colonial leaders including Adams, Jay, and Hamilton understood the danger precisely. If Parliament could establish this kind of system in Quebec, nothing prevented it from applying the same logic southward. The expansion of Canadian territory also closed off the westward land colonists believed was essential to their future growth and prosperity, a grievance that connected directly to earlier complaints about the Proclamation Line. Dallas Terry of the Kinder Institute at the University of Missouri explains why the Declaration called the Quebec Act an "example and fit instrument" for introducing absolute rule, and why proximity transformed a law about Canada into a direct threat to colonial liberty. Episode 20 of our 27-part series is live. Join us for Constitution Day Live:  https://bit.ly/4fnlTXp  #AmericanHistory  #CivicsEducation  #HistoryTeachers  #QuebecAct

A right the British called a "second Magna Carta." A clause Lincoln suspended at the start of the Civil War. A tension the United States has never fully resolved. That's the constitutional arc of Grievance 19. Habeas corpus is the right to appear before a judge and hear the charges against you. It had protected English subjects against arbitrary detention since the Magna Carta. The colonists discovered that protection had limits. 1000s of American patriots were transported far from home without due process. Garrett Jones, Postdoc Fellow at the Thomas Jefferson Center at the University of Texas at Austin, explains that Grievance 19 produced the Suspension Clause of the U. S. Constitution. That clause has sat at the center of some of the most consequential debates in American constitutional history. Most famously, Lincoln invoked it unilaterally at the start of the Civil War, after shots were fired on Fort Sumter. How do you balance liberty and security in moments of genuine crisis? Grievance 19 opens that question, and keeps it open. Discover more and join us for Constitution Day Live:  https://bit.ly/42cJB0M New episodes every Monday, Wednesday, and Friday.  #DeclarationOfIndependence  #AmericanHistory  #CivicsEducation  #HistoryTeachers  #HabeasCorpus
